data.title: 2 Differentiated Approaches To Commodity Investing
data.description:
By Ayush Babel & Jeremy Schwartz, CFA

Commodities have been on a roll over the past few months, with strong and persistent trends emerging across several sectors. Performance, however, has been uneven beneath the surface.

Precious metals have led the rally over the past six months, significantly outperforming other commodity sectors. From late June through year-end 2025, the Bloomberg Precious Metals Subindex gained over 40%, driven by heightened geopolitical risks, strong central bank demand, and sustained investor appetite for safe-haven assets.

Industrial metals also delivered solid gains, rising around 10% over the same period, supported by structural demand from electrification, infrastructure spending, and supply constraints in key metals such as copper. In contrast, energy was the clear laggard, with the Bloomberg Energy Subindex down roughly 11% in the second half of 2025. Softer demand expectations, ample supply conditions, and persistent weakness in natural gas outweighed geopolitical risk premia that had previously supported prices. Going forward, our geopolitical outlook indicates relatively limited upside for oil.

Overall, the dispersion across commodity sectors highlights the need for diversification while maintaining strategic and tactical tilts and harnessing the trends that exist in commodity markets.

WisdomTree's two differentiated offerings in commodities navigated complex commodity cycles using very different approaches. The WisdomTree Inflation Plus Fund (WTIP) delivered positive returns in every full month of its live history; likewise, the WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC) posted positive returns in each month during the second half of last year. The two ETFs outperformed the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) by more than 6% during the period despite taking very different approaches.

Performance Comparison

The WisdomTree Inflation Plus Fund

WTIP is built on a simple but powerful objective: to provide a robust and comprehensive hedge against inflation. The strategy combines commodity exposure with U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS). While TIPS are designed to hedge expected inflation and tend to respond with a lag, commodities have historically been effective at hedging unexpected inflation and often lead inflationary regimes. By combining these two asset classes, WTIP seeks to deliver a more complete and balanced inflation hedge.

Commodity markets are inherently cyclical, often experiencing extended uptrends followed by prolonged periods of mean reversion. To improve return consistency across these cycles, WTIP employs systematic, momentum-based models that evaluate each commodity relative to its own price history to determine whether to take a long or short position. To enhance robustness, two independent momentum signals—moving-average crossover and trend breadth—are used, with positions initiated only when both signals align in the same direction. Recognizing the structural role of precious metals in investor portfolios, gold and silver are maintained as permanent long exposures in fixed, equal proportions.

The strategy is further enhanced through the use of a maximum-carry contract selection process, investing in futures contracts with the highest implied carry rather than defaulting to front-month contracts. This approach seeks to capture roll returns that are often missed by traditional commodity benchmarks.

The commodity allocation within WTIP is structured as follows:

  • 80% Momentum-Based Commodity Exposure
    • Sixteen commodity futures are assessed and assigned a long (+), short (-), or flat (0) signal
    • Total exposure is equally distributed across commodities with active long or short signals
    • For example, if 10 commodities generate a long or short signal, each position receives an 8% allocation
  • Structural Long Allocation to Precious Metals
    • 7.5% allocated to gold
    • 7.5% allocated to silver

In addition, 5% of the portfolio is allocated to bitcoin exposure, implemented through a momentum-driven model, reflecting its potential role as an alternative, inflation-sensitive asset.

So far, the strategy has successfully navigated the commodity markets and harnessed the trend to deliver positive returns in all six complete months since its launch (Jul 2025 to Dec 2025). By contrast, the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) was up in only four of the six months. Overall, WTIP (+14.42%) outperformed BCOM (+7.52%) by 6.91 percentage points during this period.

The WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund

The WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund is a forward-looking, strategic commodity allocation designed to harness the historically low correlation of commodities with traditional asset classes, helping to enhance diversification within multi-asset portfolios.

Each year, WisdomTree's investment team, in collaboration with in-house commodity specialists, sets the fund's strategic commodity weights based on a long-term market outlook. This strategic allocation is complemented by a second layer of monthly rebalancing, which allows the portfolio to adapt to evolving market conditions through tactical tilts around the strategic weights.

The strategic framework seeks broad diversification while expressing conviction in the key structural themes shaping commodity markets. Currently, the portfolio is positioned around four core pillars:

  • Underweight energy relative to major commodity benchmarks, reflecting the sector's higher volatility, its tendency to dominate overall portfolio risk, and the ongoing transition toward alternative energy sources.
  • Overweight precious metals as a more defensive allocation and a potential inflation-sensitive diversifier.
  • Overweight industrial metals to capture demand linked to electrification and other future-oriented technologies, including copper, aluminum, nickel and tin.
  • Measured bitcoin exposure, with up to 10% allocated to ETPs that primarily hold bitcoin futures, recognizing its growing role as an alternative, non-traditional asset.

In addition, the fund employs an enhanced roll methodology, investing in futures contracts with the highest implied carry rather than defaulting to front-month contracts. By selectively investing further out on the futures curve, the strategy seeks to enhance returns while helping to reduce short-term volatility.

Overall, this approach has also delivered strong recent performance, with positive returns in each month of the second half of last year, outperforming BCOM by over 6% during the period.

Conclusion

WTIP and GCC represent two distinct yet complementary approaches to commodity investing, each designed to address different investor objectives.

WTIP is purpose-built as a comprehensive inflation hedge, combining the defensive properties of TIPS with the ability of commodities to respond to unexpected inflation. Its momentum-driven, long–short commodity framework, enhanced by dual trend signals, dynamic contract selection and structural allocations to precious metals and bitcoin, allows it to actively navigate commodity cycles and reduce/reverse the impact of prolonged drawdowns. The strategy's strong performance since launch highlights the benefits of an adaptive, rules-based approach in volatile inflationary environments.

GCC, in contrast, serves as a strategic, forward-looking commodity allocation. Through a combination of annually set strategic weights, monthly tactical rebalancing and an enhanced roll methodology, GCC seeks to capture long-term commodity themes while managing risk and volatility. Its emphasis on industrial metals, precious metals and reduced energy exposure reflects structural shifts such as electrification and energy transition, while maintaining the diversification benefits commodities offer.

In summary, WTIP is best suited for investors seeking an active inflation-focused solution aimed at harnessing trends in commodity markets and delivering consistent returns with lower risk, whereas GCC offers a strategic, diversified commodity exposure aligned with long-term thematic drivers. Together, they demonstrate how different portfolio objectives can be addressed through thoughtful and differentiated commodity strategy design.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing including possible loss of principal. Because of the frequency with which the Funds expect to roll futures contracts, the impact of such contango or backwardation may be greater than the impact would be if the Funds experienced less portfolio turnover. Please read the Funds’ prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund's risk profile.

GCC: An investment in this Fund is speculative, involves a substantial degree of risk, and should not constitute an investor's entire portfolio. One of the risks associated with the Fund is the complexity of the different factors which contribute to the Fund's performance. These factors include use of commodity futures contracts. In addition, bitcoin and bitcoin futures are a relatively new asset class. They are subject to unique and substantial risks and, historically, have been subject to significant price volatility. While the bitcoin futures market has grown substantially since bitcoin futures commenced trading, there can be no assurance that this growth will continue. In addition, derivatives can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effects of varied economic conditions. The value of the shares of the Fund relate directly to the value of the futures contracts and other assets held by the Fund and any fluctuation in the value of these assets could adversely affect an investment in the Fund's shares. Because of the frequency with which the Fund expects to roll futures contracts, the price of futures contracts further from expiration may be higher (a condition known as "contango") or lower (a condition known as "backwardation") and the impact of such contango or backwardation may be greater than the impact would be if the Fund experienced less portfolio turnover.

WTIP: Inflation-protected U.S. Treasury Bonds (“TIPS”) can provide a hedge against inflation, as the inflation adjustment feature helps preserve the purchasing power of the investment. Because of this inflation adjustment feature, inflation-protected bonds typically have lower yields than conventional fixed rate bonds and will likely decline in price during periods of deflation, which could result in losses. Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, credit, inflation, and reinvestment risks. Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of fixed-income securities falls. The value of commodities and commodity-linked derivative instruments typically is based upon the price movements in other asset classes. An active trading market may not exist for certain commodities. The Fund is subject to risks related to rolling futures contracts. The price of futures contracts further from expiration may be higher (“contango”) or lower (“backwardation”), which can impact the Fund’s returns. Because of the frequency with which the Fund expects to roll futures contracts, the impact of such contango or backwardation may be greater than the impact would be if the Fund experienced less portfolio turnover. In addition, bitcoin and bitcoin futures are a relatively new asset class. They are subject to unique and substantial risks and, historically, have been subject to significant price volatility. While the bitcoin futures market has grown substantially since bitcoin futures commenced trading, there can be no assurance that this growth will continue. The Fund may invest in the WisdomTree Bitcoin Fund, a bitcoin exchange-traded product, sponsored by an affiliate of the Fund’s adviser.

Ayush Babel, Director, Quantitative Research

Ayush Babel is the Director of Quantitative Research in WisdomTree's multi-asset quantitative research and index teams. In this role, he focuses on developing innovative quantitative strategies across various asset classes while supporting WisdomTree's diverse range of products. His expertise spans factor exploration, portfolio construction and optimization, quantitative investment research, and product development.

With over a decade of experience in the financial services industry, Ayush has held investment research roles at J.P. Morgan and Franklin Templeton. At these institutions, he was responsible for developing and managing equity and fixed income smart beta products, as well as cross-asset risk premia solutions for global institutional and retail clients. His experience covers a broad spectrum of asset classes and investment styles.

Ayush holds a Bachelor's in Engineering Physics and a master’s degree in Nanoscience from the Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree’s investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree’s equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper “What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?” He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.

data.title: LXP Industrial Trust: Solid Fundamentals, But Better Risk-Adjusted Income From Preferred
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

LXP Industrial Trust (LXP) recovered about 25% over the past year, currently trading around what seems to be a fair value, while the company's preferred stock still seems to offer an opportunity to get a relatively safe, reliable, and high yield.

As a quick introduction, since we're talking about a less popular company, LXP is a pure-play industrial REIT owning 112 new Class A bulk distribution properties, focusing on 12 markets across the Sunbelt and lower Midwest, with an average age of properties of 9.8 years.

Their tenants include Amazon (AMZN) as the largest, with 6.9% of ABR, Nissan in number two, accounting for 4.8% of ABR, Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) with 3.6%, and Walmart (WMT) with 3.3%.

LXP reported an overall mixed Q3, with FFO in line with the market's expectations but a slight revenue miss, with very small growth YoY, including for the 9-month period.

Note that the company's Adjusted Company FFO is not the same as the regular AFFO, as they don't include things like straight-line rent, recurring CAPEX, tenant improvements, and leasing commissions, which should result in a roughly 15% lower AFFO, meaning roughly $40 million in Q3 and ~$119.3 million over the 9M'25 period, or about $160 million per year when annualizing it.

Financially, based on LXP's latest report, we see an overall solid position for a REIT, while they also had quite a significant amount of cash on hand and an overall low amount of debt and leverage.

Although the ~$2.07 billion equity is far from their $3.03 billion market cap, I'll note again that these assets are not only depreciated for tax reasons, but they are also recorded at the value of acquisition, and as you'll see in a couple of paragraphs, they can prove to have significantly higher values even in today's market.

As we can see, their leverage decreased significantly from a year ago, while the debt comes at a very manageable ~3.63% rate and is spread very well throughout the future, with no maturity until 2027.

Recently, LXP announced closing on their $600 million revolving credit facility (SOFR + 77.5 basis points vs previous + 95 b.p.) and a $250 million unsecured term loan used to refinance their January 2027 loan ($300 million term loan), with a new maturity in January 2029 and a rate of SOFR plus 85 b.p. compared to SOFR plus 110 b.p.

Meanwhile, LXP also announced the sale of two vacant development projects in Florida and Indianapolis for ~$175 million (20% premium to gross book value), resulting in "immediate earnings accretion" of ~6% to adjusted FFO per share while also reducing their leverage significantly, from 5.8x net debt to adjusted EBITDA to 5.2x.

As they say, this is also estimated to increase the occupancy of their Stabilized Portfolio to 96.8%, while they also acquired a $30 million property at a cash cap rate of 6.5%. LXP's Chairman, President, and CEO highlighted the following during their Q3 Earnings Call:

In closing, we're pleased with our third quarter results and our outlook moving forward. The accretive sale of the vacant development projects addressed our most important operating objectives in 2025 and positions LXP for a strong 2026 and beyond. We remain focused on creating value for our shareholders by marking rents to market, raising rents through annual escalators, capitalizing on our lease-up opportunities and concentrating on our 12-market investment strategy.

LXP is set to announce their Q4 Earnings Report on February 12, with the market expecting a slight improvement in normalized FFO of $0.82 and slightly lower revenue of $86.05 million. Since it's the final report for 2025, this one should carry a bit more importance than usual, as I'm looking forward to the company providing guidance or at least some insights into 2026, so we could see more volatility as a result.

Dividends & Buybacks

LXP currently pays a ~5.46% dividend yield, recently increasing it by 3.7%. However, note that this costs about $165.44 million, which is right on the edge of the estimated AFFO mentioned above, although it's worth noting that the transaction mentioned above should bridge this gap, although we're still talking about a very elevated payout ratio, which exposes them to further economic pressure.

On the other hand, there's also the company's convertible preferred stock (LXP.PR.C) paying an even better ~6.83% yield, which is much safer, with a 6.5% initial coupon rate. I believe this is overall a safer way to get a solid yield in this industry, with an extremely low call risk (in fact, it can't be called but can be forced converted), with a conversion price of ~$102.7 following the reverse split, as the common would have to more than double before being able to force the conversion.

Risks & Opportunities

Right now, the cost of capital remains the main reason behind the weakness seen in REITs, being caused by the high-rate environment as the Fed is trying to fight inflation through rate hikes. After all, the recent volatility and weakness in the stock are not unique to LXP - far from it.

As mentioned above, the recent $30 million acquisition came with a 6.5% cap rate, and even though it's true that this will be subject to rent escalators over the long term (2.9% average rent escalators) and the property's value should also grow given enough time, we've also seen above how expensive their debt and equity can be. Meanwhile, asset recycling like the two properties they sold recently could work, but there's only so much you can recycle at a given time.

Although there is potential from rate cuts, we should keep in mind that not only did we see a very divided FOMC meeting back in December, with a 9-3 vote in favor of the cut (most divided since 2019), but the long-term rates that influence the SOFR are ultimately limited by the market's expectations surrounding the country's economy.

And that's ultimately an issue now, with the rising political, social, economic, etc. uncertainty following all the recent events. Right now, with the market's tariff/trade war fears have just been reignited over the weekend following Trump's announcement of a new round of tariffs due to his plans for Greenland and the EU preparing a response that would allow reciprocal tariffs estimated at $108 billion, while the country's traditional allies seem to move more towards each other or even China.

Due to all the uncertainty around Greenland, Venezuela, oil, tariffs, and significant consumer pressure, especially for lower-income households, we should be aware that there is potential to see higher rates for longer even if the Fed continues cutting.

Regarding the Fed, there's also rising fear around its independence, as Powell's term ends in May 2026, and Trump will likely nominate a person that shares his views, taking away what I consider to be an important "pillar of predictability" that was at least helping the market moderate its near-term expectations.

Meanwhile, there are also positives, as the company and the asset class in general can stand to benefit when the environment potentially improves, although the timing will remain uncertain.

Virtually all main real estate branches across the US saw a boom shortly after the pandemic, followed by what can be called a bust now due to rising construction costs (materials, workforce, debt) being met with falling demand and rising macro pressure from occupancy issues.

Also, as LXP also highlights, they stand to benefit from the fact that ~64% of their leases will expire through 2030, estimating the current mark-to-market on rent to be at around 17%, offering a potential opportunity for organic growth even if they don't develop due to a weak environment, although they remain focused on continuing to take advantage of potential opportunities.

Risks Reiterated

Despite significant potential to improve over the following years as they could see a "perfect storm" of macro improvements combined with re-leasing at significantly better rates, LXP can still face macro pressure, which can directly affect their cost of external capital and ability to recycle assets, slowing their growth potential, while the high lease expirations over the following years can also be a risk.

Meanwhile, although the dividend can be paid and they have plenty of cash on the balance sheet to cover it for some years if there's no recovery, a cut would definitely have significant consequences on the common, while the tenant concentration is also a risk to keep in mind despite their quality.

Valuation

For LXP's valuation, I'll start with the estimated AFFO of $160 million mentioned above, which would grow at a CAGR of 6% (similar to their accretive FFO estimate and not far from the market's consensus FFO estimate during this time) as they can benefit significantly from re-leasing, potential asset sales and better costs of capital as the environment improves, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years since we're still talking about a relatively small company that has potential to expand (on such a low number of properties, any property technically adds a lot, so it's easier to find accretive acquisitions), followed by a 2.75% terminal growth rate, a 9% discount rate and 59.48 million shares outstanding as of their Q3 report (following the reverse split).

This way, we get to an equity value of $3.15 billion, for an intrinsic value of $52.98 per share, being very close to the current $51.28 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusion

I rate LXP as a Hold since it appears to be fairly valued for my taste, while LXP.PR.C is a Buy, backed by a solid and relatively safe yield and vital assets in high-potential regions, while the business can benefit from long-term macro tailwinds.

Of course, there are risks to consider, and given the risk-reward, I don't see that many reasons to go for the company's common stock in this market.

data.title: Houston, We Have An Index: Exploring The S&P Kensho Global Space Index
data.description:
In 2025, the S&P Kensho Global Space Index ended 2025 up 57.79%, outperforming both the S&P 500® (up 17.66%) and the S&P Global BMI (up 21.75%). The strong performance was not unique to last year; it has outperformed over the past four years, and it outperformed in annualized terms over the past three and five years (see Exhibit 1).

This positive performance reflects the growing relevance of the space segment. In fact, by 2035, the space economy is expected to reach USD 1.8 trillion,1 which is roughly equivalent to South Korea’s GDP. In recent years, the industry has been undergoing a transition from government-dominated funding to an evolving private sector participation that has propelled technological breakthroughs,2 like reusable launch vehicles. These developments have contributed to lower operational costs and higher ROI, 3 specially for sending spacecraft into orbit. As a result, the industry is witnessing new commercial opportunities,4 from cutting-edge satellite communication services to leasing space stations for biotech or materials research, transforming it into a hub for diverse business opportunities.

In this diverse and emerging industry, many firms have diversified business involvements that extend beyond space-related ventures. For example, Boeing, while known for manufacturing airplanes, has begun collaborating with NASA to make rockets.5 Because of these nuances, the S&P Kensho Global Space Index uses a natural language processing (NLP)-based approach that analyzes company descriptions and filings to identify firms that are focused on space-related activities. It includes not only businesses involved with spacecrafts, launch vehicles or space stations, but also those supplying critical components, services and infrastructure. Moreover, the index may feature companies participating in satellite technology, asteroid mining and military space advancements. For more information, refer to the index methodology.

Companies in the Aerospace & Defense industry of the GICS classification are frequently associated with the space theme. In fact, 57.6% of the weight of the S&P Kensho Global Space Index was classified under this industry as of Dec. 31, 2025 (see Exhibit 2). However, if we look into RBICS data, a classification system that is based on revenue sources, space-related equities do not necessarily rely only on income streams exclusively associated with defense (see Exhibit 3).

Interestingly, in a year in which defense stocks were among the best-performing industries globally, the space theme fared better. In 2025, the S&P Kensho Global Space Index outperformed the S&P Global BMI Aerospace & Defense (Industry), which was up 49.66% (see Exhibit 4). From the 18.1% surge that the space-focused index had from August 2025 to year-end 2025, more than half of the performance was attributed to five companies that are not classified within the Aerospace & Defense industry, demonstrating that the space theme’s growth is not solely dependent on defense.

Looking at the sources of revenue that contributed the most to the S&P Kensho Global Space Index’s 57.79% performance, RBICS data shows that, among the top 10 contributors, 7 are not uniquely linked to the defense domain (see Exhibit 5). This highlights the emergence of space as a standalone theme that goes beyond traditional defense applications.

In recent years, the S&P Kensho Global Space Index has performed well. Due to its thematic approach, it can be used as the spaceship to navigate the complex galaxy of the space economy and its participants, characterized by diverse business models, emerging technologies and multifaceted revenue streams.

1 Space: The $1.8 Trillion Opportunity for Global Economic Growth – INSIGHT REPORT – APRIL 2024 – World Economic Forum

2 Space tech: Experts name 12 transformative technologies reshaping our cosmic future – World Economic Forum

3 The Space Boom Is Here – Global Finance Magazine

4 A Guide to Identifying Business Opportunities in the Space Economy – New Space Economy

5 The past, present, and future of Boeing in space – Astronomy.com

Disclosure: Copyright © 2026 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

data.title: Alamos Gold Wants To Satisfy The Market With More Value
data.description:
Alamos Gold Inc.: The Opportunity in the Gold Stock Market

In the current upward trend of the gold price, an investment in the NYSE-listed shares of Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI), a Canadian gold producer with operations in North America and Mexico, appears to be a sensible move. In addition to the record price of gold, a combination of other factors is likely to drive valuations even higher, thereby reinforcing the strong positive correlation with rising gold prices.

The Favourable Context of the Bullish Gold Price

Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) has risen by 72% in the last twelve months and by 8% since the beginning of 2026, currently standing at $4,668.387 per troy ounce. Macroeconomic factors such as renewed geopolitical tensions—particularly the conflict between the US and the EU elite over Greenland's mineral resources, fueled by Macron's proposal for a massive trade offensive in response to Trump's threat of a 10 percent tariff on EU products—are driving demand for gold as a safe haven. This demand is now further amplified by perceived financial risks in the US banking system.

As Yahoo Finance reported last Wednesday (source already linked just above), Ulrike Hoffmann-Burchardi, CIO Americas and Global Head of Equities at UBS Global Wealth Management, issued a remarkable gold price forecast of $5,000 per ounce for the coming months, or even $5,400 per ounce.

A Solid Positive Correlation Between Alamos and Gold

The positive correlation between the NYSE shares of Alamos Gold Inc. (symbol: AGI) and changes in the gold price is illustrated by the dark yellow curve in the second box below the main chart. This curve shows AGI's return relative to the gold price over the past year. The dark yellow area in this box has generally been positive.

This suggests that AGI shares, on average, tend to be bullish when the price of gold rises and bearish when the price of gold falls, regardless of the returns on AGI shares and the price of gold over the past year (as these returns may well vary widely despite the positive correlation between the two securities). This pattern arises because fluctuations in the gold price influence market expectations about AGI's future profitability, which, in turn, plays a key role in shaping market sentiment on North American stock exchanges.

Alamos Gold Inc.: Canadian Gold Producer in America

In its production report for the fourth quarter and full year 2025 dated January 14, 2026 (hereinafter referred to as the “AGI Production Report”), Alamos Gold Inc. describes itself as follows:

Alamos is a Canadian-based intermediate gold producer with diversified production from three operations in North America. This includes the Young-Davidson mine and Island Gold District in northern Ontario, Canada, and the Mulatos District in Sonora State, Mexico. Additionally, the Company has a strong portfolio of growth projects, including the Phase 3+ Expansion at Island Gold, and the Lynn Lake project in Manitoba, Canada. […].

Alamos Gold Inc. is the sole owner of the following gold mines:

  • Young-Davidson Mine, an underground gold mine, 75 km southeast of Timmins, Ontario, Canada.
  • Island Gold Mine, an underground gold mine, 10 km southeast of Dubreuilville, Ontario, Canada.
  • Mulatos Mine, an open-pit leaching gold mine, 172 km northeast of Ciudad Obregón, Sonora, Mexico.
  • Lynn Lake Project, open-pit feasibility study, proposed gold and silver mine, 8 km northeast of Lynn Lake, Manitoba, Canada.

Key findings of the feasibility study—2023 forecast (according to the Alamos Gold website): The Lynn Lake project is projected to achieve an average annual production of 176,000 ounces of gold over the first 10 years. Based on our experience with other gold mining projects, Lynn Lake will enable low-cost gold production with all-in sustaining costs (AISC) of US$699 per ounce. Mineral resource information is also available on the website. The forecast is based on proven and probable mineral reserves of 3,322,000 ounces and measured and indicated mineral resources of 587,000 ounces. Lynn Lake could sell its first gold in early 2029. A higher initial capital cost (as seen in the earnings call transcript: Alamos Gold Q3 2025 from Investing.com), primarily due to inflation in recent years, does not seem to deter the company, which, on the contrary, has not reduced shareholder value programs since October 2025 and continues to have a strong focus on returning to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks. Existing liquidity is ample, and multi-year cash flows - assuming the gold price remains at its current peak - will be robust, and these two things lead us to believe that banks will be willing to extend existing credit lines or provide new loans if necessary.

Alamos Gold Q4 & 2025 Production: Trends & Drivers

Gold production did not increase from the previous quarter but remained at the high level of Q3 2025. This interruption of growth disappointed the market, but we see no reason to overly penalize AGI because of this.

Although the company finished 2025 below its forecasts, this is a minor, temporary deviation attributable to uncontrollable external factors, particularly the extreme weather conditions in December. Despite these disruptions, the company's business remains unaffected, and it is well-positioned to benefit from the ongoing, record-breaking gold price rally.

John A. McCluskey, President and CEO of Alamos Gold Inc., commented on the production results for the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025 as follows:

Our fourth quarter production totaled 141,500 ounces, consistent with the third quarter but below guidance due to lower than planned production from our Canadian operations. Mining and processing rates were lower than expected due to severe winter weather late in December which impacted access to the sites, as well as other operational challenges. Despite these challenges, we delivered a record quarter and year financially and made significant progress on our growth initiatives,

The company's production report indicates that while there are temporary difficulties, these will turn into upward potential once they are resolved.

From Alamos Gold's production report, we understand that:

Young-Davidson is on track to meet its gold production forecasts again as issues related to winter storms have been resolved, the damaged underground paste plug facility has been repaired, and capacity expansion through a new ore shaft will be completed in early 2026.

Island Gold sees operational benefits on the horizon, as the effects of the October 2025 earthquake have been overcome and the processing plant capacity is increased to 20,000 tonnes per day, leading to significant savings in operating costs, particularly in energy consumption.

Despite increased targets, production in the Mulatos district met the annual forecasts for 2025, underlining Mulatos' strong performance, with La Yaqui Grande exceeding planned results.

Young-Davidson, Island Gold, and Mulatos' “La Yaqui Grande” inspire hope for optimism regarding future productions.

Production Outlook

In his comments on the 2025 production results, John A. McCluskey explained his vision for the future growth of gold production:

From an operational perspective, this past year was not reflective of our long-term track record, nor our strong outlook. We expect a substantial improvement from our operations in 2026 and low-cost growth from the Island Gold District to drive production significantly higher over the next several years. As we will be outlining in the Island Gold District Expansion Study next month, we expect this growth to continue with a clear path to one million ounces of annual production by the end of the decade,

Financial Figures as of the End of 2025

Despite the aforementioned operational setbacks, Alamos Gold achieved record sales of $568 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 and record annual sales of $1.8 billion. These figures would be even more impressive if the realized gold price were not capped, as the company states, by the following:

the gold prepayment facility executed in July 2024 based on the prepaid price of $2,524 per ounce.

Margin improvement and cash flow were robust, allowing Alamos to achieve the major improvements that excite the market because they are synonymous with growth: the company ended the year with $623 million in cash and cash equivalents (up 35% sequentially and 90% year-over-year) and reduced its outstanding debt to $200 million. Alamos had sufficient free cash flow to fund its shareholder value enhancement program and market returns. For the full year, the company paid approximately $42.2 million in quarterly dividends, or $0.025 per share (the most recent payment was made on December 18, 2025). Also, 1,326,929 shares were repurchased for $38.8 million, resulting in a total payout to shareholders of $81 million for 2025, as we can see from the company's report.

For Investors: What to Expect from Alamos Gold

Resolving the operational issues will pave the way for higher production than projected for 2025, while costs will decrease by spreading fixed costs over a larger base and achieving economies of scale. Analysts also predict that the price of gold could rise to $5,400 per ounce in the coming months. These factors, together with the gradual phasing-out of half of Argonaut's legacy gold hedging transactions in 2026, increase the balance sheet's potential to benefit from a record rise in the gold price. Strictly speaking, the balance sheet and shareholder value must not be in worse shape at the end of 2026 than they were in 2025.

This exposure will be strengthened as the company continues to seek to acquire the remaining Argonaut legacy hedging positions, totaling 100,000 ounces for the period from late 2026 to early 2027, out of a total of 330,000 ounces, as 230,000 ounces have already been eliminated.

Alamos Gold Inc. on the NYSE

Alamos Gold Inc.’s NYSE-listed shares under the symbol “AGI” were trading at $38.90/share, giving it a market capitalization of $16.33 billion, as of this writing.

The stock offers a Div Rate (FWD) of $0.10, leading to a Yield (FWD) of 0.26%, according to Seeking Alpha.

Technical Analysis

The current stock price is 18.3% above its 52-week moving average of $31.775 (range: $19.68 to $43.87). The following structure—the 20-day moving average above the 50-day moving average, which in turn is above the 200-day moving average—suggests that AGI stock is in a sustained uptrend in both the short and long term.

Valuation

The company states:

Increased shareholder returns: repurchased 928,729 shares in November at a cost of $28.8 million, or $30.96 per share. For the full year, 1,326,929 shares were repurchased for $38.8 million. Including dividends and share buybacks, a record $81 million was returned to shareholders in 2025

The average repurchase price was $30.96 per share in November 2025 and $29.24 per share for the full year 2025. From December 31, 2024, to December 31, 2025, AGI's average share price on the NYSE was approximately $28.24. The increase in market capitalization was strong, driven by the record increase in the price of gold. The company readily bought back its own shares, resulting in higher expenses and a higher average cash payout per share. Alamos Gold Inc. will have more funds available for share repurchases and maintaining dividend payments going forward as the gold business is on a solid footing, thanks to improved gold production, lower costs, and record gold prices, which is why this stock is a buy, in our view. Alamos Gold plans to repurchase more shares between December 24, 2025, and December 23, 2026, than in 2025 under its previous share repurchase program: 18.58 million Class A shares versus 1.33 million shares for C$54.4 million at an average price of C$40.97 per share or approximately $29.7542 on December 23, 2025. If the company continues to implement and enhance this share repurchase program despite the expected strong share price appreciation due to the positive correlation with the price of gold, we believe that these market valuations of AGI shares on the NYSE represent a buying opportunity relative to the value the company will return to the market in the near future as its business will generate robust cash flows. Alamos Gold has received approval from the Toronto Stock Exchange (TSX) to repurchase its own shares. The positive effects of this strategy are expected to be felt not only on the TSX but also on the NYSE, as this share buyback program will be possible due to the continued strong cash flow or value.

The Risk

The assets are located in mining-friendly regions and do not pose a material threat to the continuation of Alamos' mining and exploration activities in Canada and Mexico. The only risk is the price of gold: While the strong correlation between AGI's share price and the price of gold could lead to price fluctuations, an abrupt trend reversal in the gold price would have a significant adverse impact on the expected growth of cash flow from the company's operating activities and on its ongoing share buyback program. This risk is very low given the current situation, as macroeconomic problems, exacerbating geopolitical tensions, and the projected interest rate cuts by the US Federal Reserve in 2026 should drive the price of gold higher going forward.

Conclusion

Alamos Gold Inc. is a Canadian gold producer with production and expansion activities in Canada and Mexico—two regions ideally suited for onshore mining and exploration. Alamos's cash flow benefits from record gold prices, rising gold production, and decreasing costs. The shares have significant upside potential, as the market will—as is typical—react positively to Alamos' plans for capital distribution through share buybacks and dividends. This is the strongest evidence of the value the company can offer in the market.

data.title: The Trade Desk: The Death Of Its Growth Is Exaggerated
data.description:
Overview

The Trade Desk (TTD) had a rough year in 2025; the stock ended ~70% lower for the year. The company achieved revenue growth rates in the second and third quarters of 2025 that were materially lower than its five-year average. Wall Street is panicking that growth will be structurally lower from now on, primarily because of an increased threat from Amazon.

Wall Street is concerned because Amazon is now selling advertisements not just on its own properties, like Amazon.com and Amazon Prime, but also on other companies' platforms. This expansion means Amazon is starting to compete more directly with TTD by moving into the same areas of digital advertising.

Although some of these arguments warrant some vigilance, I am bullish on the stock. Amazon is not a full substitute for TTD because TTD has two main advantages: its neutrality and its breadth. Additionally, CTV advertising expenditure, where TTD makes 50% of its money, is nowhere near saturated, and audio is becoming the next big growth driver.

Company Overview

TTD is a platform where advertisers can buy ads. It is the biggest independent ad-buying platform where advertisers buy ads on streaming television, audio, display ads, and in-app advertising. TTD doesn't own any content; it's merely a technology platform, and it earns money by charging a take rate on the advertising spend on its platform.

The two main competitors are Google's DV360 and Amazon's ad buying platform called Amazon DSP (demand-side platform, a platform to buy ads). As I will show in this article, TTD has distinct advantages against these two because of its breadth and its neutrality.

Growth Appears to Be Hit by Macro Headwinds

As you can see from the chart below, revenue growth bottomed out in the third quarter of 2025 at 17.7%. In Q2 it was slightly higher. The median revenue growth rate over the last five years was 25%, so this is the reason Wall Street is nervous.

If we listen carefully to Jeff Green, CEO of TTD, he does mention that advertising spend is influenced by macro volatility. Per Q2 2025 earnings call:

"From a macro standpoint, some of the world's largest brands are absolutely facing pressure and a degree of uncertainty. Some must respond more than others to tariffs. Many are managing inflation worries and the related pricing that comes with that."

In Q3 2025, he mentions that "especially retail and consumer products are still feeling pressure from inflation and tariffs."

TTD is a company that is sensitive to macro shocks because the spending that happens on their platform is discretionary. The nature of CTV advertising on the platform is that brands want to create brand awareness and reach. Like advertisements on analog TV, the return on investment is not instant but shows up over time.

This is different from Google Search ads or Amazon.com ads, where users show clear buying intent. If an advertiser were to stop advertising spending on Google Search or Amazon ads, sales would probably immediately be affected.

But with TTD, that is not immediately the case, so it's easier for CFOs or chief marketing officers to hold off on spending on TTD's platform if macro uncertainties arise.

Furthermore, TTD has shown sensitivity to macro shocks in the past as well. The prime example is in Q2 2020, when revenue growth suddenly contracted by -13%. That was also a situation where TTD reacted more heavily to macro shocks than Google or Meta. Google's revenue fell 1.7% that quarter, while Meta's grew 10.7%.

The last crucial point is that, excluding political advertising spending in Q3 2025, the revenue growth rate would be 22%. Excluding political spending makes sense because revenue was artificially high in Q3 2024 due to the US elections. A 22% growth rate would be perfectly normal for Trade Desk.

For me, the explanation that management has given seems plausible, given the nature of the business and historical sensitivity to macro shocks.

The CTV Engine is Still Running

Let's look at TTD's revenue growth drivers. CTV (connected television or streaming TV) advertising makes up about half of TTD's business, so it's important to track its status. The graph below shows that CTV ads account for 32% of total US TV spending. Compare that to 40 to 60 percent of users already watching CTV rather than analog TV, and the takeaway is that most TV budgets still need to migrate to CTV, which will benefit TTD.

Looking at the chart, an additional $5 billion annual spend will move over from linear TV spending to CTV. That is $5 billion potential to flow through TTD's platform. On a trailing 12-month revenue of $2.8 billion, $5 billion of potential spend can be a significant revenue driver.

Two main reasons that support the increase in CTV advertising spend are:

  • CTV players like Disney and Netflix have successfully introduced ad tiers. That increases (premium) CTV ad availability and may attract more new advertisers.
  • CTV advertising is also becoming much more performance-based, meaning that the return on investment is becoming much clearer versus traditional TV advertising. For example, by using TTD's developed Unified ID technology, advertisers can see if a user who watched a CTV advertisement also bought something at Walmart. That's because Unified ID is a method where email addresses get encrypted and tokenized so that offline purchases can be linked to a CTV ad, usually by linking the logged-in email address of the CTV service with an offline loyalty card.

All taken together, the remaining market opportunity, continued growth in ad-tier models, and better performance measures all support continued growth in CTV advertising.

Joint Business Plans at All-Time Highs

Other evidence of future growth is indicated by CEO Jeff Green's statement that joint business plans (JBP) are at record highs, as mentioned in the Q3 2025 earnings call:

"We are signing more multiyear JBPs, or joint business plans, than ever before with leading agencies and brands. In fact, the number of live JBPs is at an all-time high, and we continue to see spending under JBPs significantly outpaced the rest of our business. What is even more encouraging is the strength of our JBP pipeline, with 100 JBPs in progress, many of them in the late stages of development."

Growth in JBPs is a bullish sign because:

  • It creates predictability on the amount of spending the customer is planning to make.
  • Increases the stickiness of the customer. By signing a JBP, it's less likely that the client is moving away advertising spend to a competitor.
  • It's usually also a sign that a brand is willing to commit its data to the TTD platform. Like the example with Walmart, where its store shopping data is used to link to CTV ads.

Audio Advertising is the Next Growth Driver for TTD

Another major growth avenue in the coming years can be audio-not traditional radio, but digital audio such as Spotify, Pandora, iHeart, and similar platforms. TTD has integrations with all of them. This market is rapidly shifting toward programmatic advertising. As recently as April 2025, Spotify introduced its Spotify Ad Exchange, enabling advertisers to bid programmatically on Spotify's audio inventory.

Specifically, demand for podcast advertising is enormous. TTD estimates that consumers spend an average of three hours a day listening to their favorite music and podcasts. Their forecast is that audio and CTV will increase as a percentage of the revenue mix.

According to Digiday, programmatic buys on Spotify alone grew 58% year over year in Q3 2024. Currently, most podcast ads are pre-recorded and therefore not tailored to the individual listener. Programmatic audio advertising enables ads to be served dynamically to specific listeners, significantly increasing value for advertisers compared to pre-recorded ads.

Considering how much time people spend listening to audio while driving, exercising, or taking a walk, I agree with Green that audio is likely to become the largest component of the revenue mix.

According to the chart below from eMarketer, programmatic audio spend is expected to reach $2.6 billion in 2026, representing a 17% CAGR since 2022.

Audio has a big future, considering the engagement audio has during the day. One research study in Australia, for example, finds that digital audio has a higher engagement than CTV:

While audio currently represents only about 5% of TTD's business, the key takeaway is that audio is likely to become a meaningful growth driver-much like CTV has been in recent years-further cementing TTD's long-term growth runway.

Amazon's Conflict of Interest is Its Weakness

A concern I often see among analysts and fellow writers is the perceived threat from Amazon's own DSP (demand-side platform) and, to a lesser extent, Google's DSP. Amazon operates its own DSP, allowing advertisers to buy ads directly through its platform. The issue is that Amazon and Google face a conflict of interest: they own both the buying platform and the inventory. Amazon simply earns more if they sell you an ad on their own platform (Amazon.com, Fire TV) compared to an external publisher because they don't have to pay an intermediary.

TTD does not have websites or CTV channels where you can buy ads. It simply routes ad spend to the placements that deliver the highest value for the advertiser. This is a key difference between Amazon and TTD and why TTD has a structural advantage.

Here are two examples where TTD's neutrality shows up in the customer experience:

  • Reporting: TTD provides log-level data for ad campaigns, offering detailed insight into when an ad was served, on which website or app, at what price, and to which ID. You can see the cost to the publisher separate from the TTD fee and any other line items. Amazon puts everything in a single figure; you can't know for sure how much of it went to a publisher and what part went to Amazon.
  • Bid Transparency: Another benefit of TTD's neutrality is that it compares your bid to the clearing price-the price actually paid for an advertisement. This lets you see how much you over- or underpaid. This is especially important because the ad tech industry has shifted to first-price auctions in recent years. This means that if bidder A pays $10 and bidder B $8, A pays $10 for the ad. In a second-price auction, A would just pay $8.01.

You can see that Amazon playing referee and player simultaneously is hurting the customers' ability to assess its ROI when buying ads.

Why TTD's Reach Matters

In addition to its neutrality, TTD profits from its fast breadth of connections with publishers worldwide. While Amazon has made deals with Netflix, Roku, and major news sites, Amazon is more selective in its reach. It can reach off-platform inventory, but it tends to focus on a smaller set of premium publishers. TTD also has a far wider reach internationally.

Its greater reach is an advantage for campaigns that need to run across multiple channels. Advertisers have a higher probability of reaching users throughout the day by combining CTV, audio, display, mobile, and other channels. With Amazon, this is harder because its (global) reach is less.

Because of its reach, TTD participates in millions of ad impressions per second, allowing it to identify cheaper ads. TTD has a better view of "ad liquidity" in the system, thereby having a better chance to retrieve the actual price of an ad. Its AI model, Kokai, has more data to work with and is able to offer the customer more efficient pricing than Amazon, which focuses on a more select set of publishers.

In short, TTD benefits from two major structural advantages: its neutrality and its breadth.

Where Amazon Does Compete

Amazon is strong when the shopper journey happens on Amazon. If a user browses on Amazon, searches for a product, or adds something to their cart, that information is valuable to an advertiser. If that same user then watches content on Prime Video and later purchases something on Amazon.com, advertisers can clearly see that the ad worked. That is gold for marketers and is known as closed-loop attribution.

However, when a user does not have a meaningful "Amazon footprint," the value of the Amazon DSP declines.

Since Amazon is still a small part of the global retail landscape, advertisers will therefore continue to use both platforms together: Amazon DSP for high-quality purchase data within the Amazon ecosystem and TTD for reaching users across the open internet.

Valuation

To assess TTD's valuation, I have used a comparison between TTD, Applovin, Meta Platforms, Alphabet, and Magnite. The reason is that all of them are active in selling ads, have a platform-like approach, and have similar margin profiles. Although Alphabet is not a 100% ad business anymore given its cloud business, it still gives a fairly good representation.

Here are the next 12-month PE ratios for these stocks:

The average PE of the peers is 27. Applying this PE to TTD's NTM earnings of $1.97 results in a justified share price of $53, a 49% upside.

But what if TTD has a lower expected earnings growth rate that warrants a lower PE ratio? Below I have listed the PE's, 5 y expected growth rates, and PEG ratio (PE NTM/5 y EPS expected growth rates)

PE NTM
